confidence.disclosure: Ibn Arabi was a meticulous record-keeper of his own life. The Futuhat al-Makkiyya is full of dated visions and named teachers and places, and his ijaza (teaching certificate) granted to al-Malik al-Muzaffar of Damascus listed his masters by city. Modern scholarship by Claude Addas (Quest for the Red Sulphur) has reconstructed his itinerary in detail. The death date of 22 Rabi al-Thani 638 AH (16 November 1240) is exact. The years of his Andalusian and Maghreb travels (1172–1200) and the eastern journey through Mecca, Anatolia, Syria and Egypt (1201–1223) are well documented; only some intermediate stops are dated by year rather than month.

sources: Ibn Arabi, Ruh al-Quds and al-Durra al-Fakhira (his memoirs of his teachers)
Claude Addas, Quest for the Red Sulphur: The Life of Ibn Arabi (1989/1993)
Stephen Hirtenstein, The Unlimited Mercifier: The Spiritual Life and Thought of Ibn Arabi (1999)
William Chittick, The Sufi Path of Knowledge (1989)

journey.event: Born on 28 July 1165 (17 Ramadan 560 AH) in Murcia, in the southeast of al-Andalus. His father Ali ibn Muhammad serves in the army of Muhammad ibn Mardanish, the independent ruler of Murcia, known to the Christians as the Wolf King.

When Murcia falls to the Almohad caliphate, Ibn Mardanish dies in defeat and Ibn Arabi's father shifts allegiance to the Almohad caliph Abu Yaqub Yusuf. The family moves to Seville, then the leading intellectual centre of the western Islamic world.

As a young man receives a conventional education in jurisprudence and hadith and is briefly secretary to the governor of Seville. Around this time he undergoes his first formative spiritual retreat — what he later calls a sudden, involuntary divine attraction rather than a disciplined ascent. It is followed by a vision of the Prophet Jesus.

His father, a friend of the philosopher Ibn Rushd (Averroes), arranges a famous meeting between the boy and the great Aristotelian. Averroes asks if reason confirms what intuition discloses; Ibn Arabi answers 'Yes — and no — and between yes and no spirits take wing from their matter and necks are severed from their bodies.' Averroes turns pale.

Marries Maryam bint Muhammad, a woman from an influential Sevillian family who shares his Sufi aspirations. Begins serious study with the Sufi masters of Andalusia, including the women saints Fatima of Cordoba and Shams of Marchena, of whom he writes lovingly in Ruh al-Quds.
Crosses to North Africa for the first time, visiting Tunis. Meets the Sufi Abdul Aziz al-Mahdawi, who becomes a lifelong friend and to whom the Futuhat al-Makkiyya will later be dedicated.

His father has died in Seville; soon afterward his mother dies. He leaves Spain again, this time travelling with his two sisters to Fez in Morocco, where he stays for several years and accepts spiritual mentorship from Mohammed ibn Qasim al-Tamimi.

Returns to Cordoba and attends the funeral of Ibn Rushd, whose body has been brought back from Marrakesh for burial in his family tomb. He watches the philosopher's books loaded onto one side of a mule and his coffin onto the other, and reflects on the imbalance.
While in Murcia he receives a commanding vision: a divine order to leave al-Andalus and travel east. He will never return.

Crosses from Gibraltar for the last time, makes his way through the Maghreb, and arrives in Cairo on his eastward journey. Visits Hebron to pay respects at the tomb of the Prophet Ibrahim and prays at the al-Aqsa Mosque in Jerusalem.

Performs the pilgrimage. In Mecca he receives 'a divine commandment' to begin his vast masterwork, al-Futuhat al-Makkiyya — The Meccan Illuminations. He begins it the same year and will work on it for nearly forty years.

Stays in Mecca for roughly three years. Befriends the family of the Persian shaykh Abu Shuja ibn Rustam, including his learned daughter Nizam, in whose beauty and intelligence Ibn Arabi sees a living embodiment of divine wisdom — his Beatrice. The poems for her become the Tarjuman al-Ashwaq.

Travels north with a group of pilgrims to the Seljuk court of Anatolia. In Konya he meets the young Sadr al-Din al-Qunawi, who will become his most important disciple and the great systematiser of his thought. He also takes the boy's mother as a wife after her husband's death.
Travels east to Baghdad, where he meets the spiritual leaders of the city.
Visits Jerusalem, Mecca and Egypt again, then passes for the first time through Syria, visiting Aleppo and Damascus.
Returns to Mecca and continues writing. The next several years are spent in constant travel back and forth between the Hijaz, Anatolia, Syria, Palestine, Iraq and Egypt.

Composes in Aleppo a sober prose commentary on his Tarjuman al-Ashwaq, after Damascus jurists had attacked the love poems for Nizam as scandalous. He insists every line is mystical allegory, and the storm passes.
Spends time at Malatya in eastern Anatolia, where his disciple Sadr al-Din al-Qunawi is based. Acts as advisor to the Seljuk sultan Kayka'us I and writes a long letter on governance that survives.

Settles permanently in Damascus, his final home, under the patronage of the wealthy Banu Zaki family. After 25 years of constant travel he dedicates the rest of his life to teaching and writing. Establishes a zawiya at the foot of Mount Qasioun.

In a dream the Prophet Muhammad appears to him holding a book and says: 'Take this book of the Fusus al-Hikam and bring it out to humanity that they may benefit from it.' Ibn Arabi composes the Fusus al-Hikam, his most concentrated and most controversial work — twenty-eight chapters, each on a prophet, each unfolding a single facet of divine wisdom.
Finalises one complete manuscript of the Futuhat al-Makkiyya. He will produce a second revision in 1238, two years before his death.
Dies in Damascus on 22 Rabi al-Thani 638 AH (16 November 1240), aged 75. Buried at the foot of Mount Qasioun. His tomb is later marked by a mosque built over it after the Ottoman conquest of Syria.
